perturbation.original_text: 1.5 "Control" means the possession, directly or indirectly, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management or policies of a person or entity, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.5 "Control" means the suggestion, directly or indirectly, of influence over the management or policies of a person or entity, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: The term "possession of the power to direct" is replaced with "suggestion of influence." This weakens the definition of control, potentially conflicting with legal interpretations used in corporate governance and securities laws, which require a more definitive level of control to trigger certain regulatory requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 2(a)(9) of the Investment Company Act of 1940
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification changes the definition of 'control' from 'the power to direct or cause the direction' to 'suggestion of influence.' This contradicts the Investment Company Act of 1940, which uses a more rigorous standard of control to determine who is subject to regulation under the Act. The modified definition makes it ambiguous whether a party actually has control, potentially allowing entities that should be regulated to avoid regulation.

perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the definition of 'control' from 'the power to direct or cause the direction' to 'suggestion of influence.' The Law Explanation states that this contradicts the Investment Company Act of 1940, which uses a more rigorous standard of control. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 80a-2, defines terms for the Investment Company Act, implying a standard for 'control' more robust than mere 'suggestion.' The modification introduces ambiguity, potentially allowing entities that should be regulated to avoid regulation. This falls under the Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 LIMITATION OF WARRANTY AND LIABILITY. LICENSOR DOES NOT MAKE WARRANTIES OF ANY KIND, WHETHER EXPRESS, IMPLIED, RELATED TO OR ARISING OUT OF THE LICENSED MARK OR THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 LIMITATION OF WARRANTY AND LIABILITY. LICENSOR MAY MAKE WARRANTIES OF ANY KIND, WHETHER EXPRESS, IMPLIED, RELATED TO OR ARISING OUT OF THE LICENSED MARK OR THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "does not make warranties" to "may make warranties" introduces ambiguity regarding whether any warranties actually exist. This directly contradicts the intent of the original clause, which was to explicitly disclaim all warranties. The change might introduce implied warranties, which are contrary to standard trademark licensing practices and potentially violate consumer protection laws if the licensed mark is used in association with goods or services.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts UCC § 2-316, which governs the exclusion or modification of warranties in sales contracts. By stating that the licensor 'may' make warranties, the clause opens the door to implied warranties, undermining the original intent to disclaim all warranties. This can create legal uncertainty, as the UCC requires specific language to effectively disclaim implied warranties, and the modified clause does not meet this requirement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces "does not make warranties" with "may make warranties," introducing ambiguity about the existence of warranties. UCC § 2-316 addresses the exclusion or modification of warranties. While the original text explicitly disclaims warranties, the modified text opens the door to implied warranties. The law snippet states that to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability, the language must mention merchantability and be conspicuous. The modified clause fails to meet this requirement because it doesn't explicitly disclaim warranties, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.10 Governing Law and Jurisdiction. The provisions of this Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California (excluding any conflict of law rule or principle that would refer to the laws of another jurisdiction). Each Party hereto irrevocably submits to the jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in California, in any action or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement, and each Party hereby irrevocably agrees that all claims in respect of any such action or proceeding must be brought and/or defended in any such court; provided, however, that matters which are under the exclusive jurisdiction of the federal courts shall be brought in the Federal District Court for the Central District of California.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.10 Governing Law and Jurisdiction. The provisions of this Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Nevada (excluding any conflict of law rule or principle that would refer to the laws of another jurisdiction). Each Party hereto may submit to the jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in California, in any action or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement, and each Party hereby agrees that all claims in respect of any such action or proceeding may be brought and/or defended in any such court; provided, however, that matters which are under the exclusive jurisdiction of the federal courts may be brought in any Federal District Court.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Nevada introduces conflict, as Nevada's laws may differ significantly on trademark licensing and contract interpretation. Replacing 'irrevocably submits' with 'may submit' and 'must' with 'may' weakens the jurisdiction clause, allowing parties to potentially litigate disputes in other jurisdictions, conflicting with the intent of a clear and binding forum selection clause. Additionally, removing the exclusive venue for federal matters introduces further jurisdictional ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change creates conflict with 28 U.S.C. § 1404(a), which governs the transfer of venue in federal courts. While parties can agree to a forum selection clause, federal courts retain the discretion to transfer a case to another district 'for the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice.' By making jurisdiction permissive rather than mandatory, the clause opens the door to forum shopping and challenges to the chosen venue, potentially undermining the enforceability of the agreement. Changing the governing law also introduces potential conflicts of law issues.

(b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in t
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., §§ 119 , 163 (Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 58, 36 Stat. 1103 ; Sept. 8, 1916, ch. 475, § 5, 39 Stat. 851 ). Section co
perturbation.justification: The change from 'irrevocably submits' to 'may submit' and 'must' to 'may' weakens the jurisdiction clause. 28 U.S.C. § 1404(a) allows a district court to transfer any civil action for convenience and justice. By making jurisdiction permissive, the clause opens the door to forum shopping, potentially undermining the agreement's enforceability. The original text mandates submission to specific courts, while the modified text allows for discretionary submission, creating a contradiction. The law snippets confirm the court's power to transfer venue under certain conditions, which the modified text exploits to create ambiguity and potential conflict.
